text: To restore communication for individuals with paralysis, speech brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) aim to translate neural activity into text or audible speech. While prior research has established the potential for such systems, achieving high-speed, large-vocabulary decoding remains a significant hurdle. In this study, we present an intracortical speech-to-text BCI that utilizes high-resolution spiking activity from microelectrode arrays. By recording from the ventral premotor cortex (area 6v) of a participant with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) who can no longer speak intelligibly, we achieved a word error rate of 9.1% on a 50-word vocabulary—a 2.7-fold improvement over previous benchmarks. More importantly, we demonstrate the first successful decoding of a 125,000-word vocabulary, achieving a word error rate of 23.8%. The BCI facilitated communication at an average speed of 62 words per minute, significantly surpassing prior records and approaching the rates of natural conversation. Our neurophysiological findings reveal that area 6v contains a robust, spatially intermixed representation of speech articulators, and that the articulatory code for phonemes remains preserved years after the loss of functional speech. These results provide a proof-of-concept for a high-performance neuroprosthesis capable of restoring rapid, unconstrained communication to people with severe speech disabilities.
